coredata.dc:title: Trace level of atomic copper in N-doped graphene quantum dots switching the selectivity from C1 to C2 products in CO electroreduction
coredata.dc:creator: Lyu, X.
Zhang, T.
Li, Z.
Jafta, C.J.
Serov, A.
Hwang, I.-H.
Sun, C.
Cullen, D.A.
Li, J.
Wu, J.
coredata.dc:description: To unravel the relationship between trace Cu on the metal-free catalysts toward CO/CO2 reduction reaction (CO/CO2RR), we investigated the effect of trace Cu loading in N-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) on CO/CO2RR. A general trend is that increasing the Cu loading in NGQDs switches the selectivity from C1 (CH4) to C2 products in CORR. When 2.5 μg/cm2 Cu with the atomic size is loaded on NGQDs, the selectivity shifts from 62% Faradaic efficiency (FE) of CH4 to 52% FE of C2 products in CORR. Further increasing the atomic Cu loading to 3.8 μg/cm2 promotes the FE of C2 products to 78%. CO2RR requires one order of magnitude higher Cu loading than CORR to switch the selectivity from C1 to C2 products due to the low partial pressure of CO. This study clarifies the distinct impact of trace (ppm level) Cu on the activity/selectivity between CORR and CO2RR.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Trace copper
Graphene quantum dots
CORR
CO2RR
Acetate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The performance of CORR over a trace Cu loaded NGQDs
The effect of Cu loadings on the selectivity in CORR
The performance of CO2RR over trace Cu loaded NGQDs
Proposed mechanism for the selectivity shift in CORR

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Office of Fossil Energy and Carbon Management
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Office of Fossil Energy and Carbon Management

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is partially supported by the Office of Fossil Energy and Carbon Management of the U.S. Department of Energy under Award Number DE-FE0031919 . This research used resources of the Advanced Photon Source, an Office of Science User Facility operated for the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) Office of Science by Argonne National Laboratory and was supported by the U.S. DOE under Contract No. DE-AC02-06CH11357 , and the Canadian Light Source and its funding partners.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The FE of all products during CORR as a function of the applied potential on the NGQD-Cu2.5 electrodes. (b) The FE of C2 products and CH4. (c) The partial current density of C2 products and CH4. The error bar represents the standard deviation from the measurements of three independent electrodes.

(a) FE and (b) partial current density of overall C2 products during CORR as a function of the applied potential on NGQD-Cu electrodes with various Cu loadings. (c) and (d) Analogous data with (a) and (b), but for CH4.

(a) The CORR product distribution as a function of the trace Cu loading. (b) The change of FEs of CH4 and C2 with the trace Cu loading. The FE data was collected at the current density of around 150 mA/cm2.
STEM image of NGQD-Cu2.5 electrode after CORR and Aberration-corrected HAADF-STEM (b) for the same electrode.

Chemical structure characterization of Cu in NGQD-Cu electrodes after CORR. (a) The fine scan XPS spectra of Cu 2p of NGQD-Cu electrodes. (b) Cu K-edge normalized XANES spectra as a function of incident photon energy for the NGQD-Cu electrodes. (c) Fourier transform of k3-weighted EXAFS of Cu in R space for the NGQD-Cu electrodes.

head.ce:title: Trace level of atomic copper in N-doped graphene quantum dots switching the selectivity from C1 to C2 products in CO electroreduction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, University of Cincinnati, Cincinnati, OH 45221, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, University of Cincinnati, Cincinnati, OH 45221, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electrification and Energy Infrastructures Division, Oak Ridge National Laboratory, Oak Ridge, TN 37831, USA
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Electrification and Energy Infrastructures Division, Oak Ridge National Laboratory; Oak Ridge, TN 37831, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: X-Ray Science Division Argonne, Argonne National Laboratory, IL 60439, USA
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: X-Ray Science Division Argonne, Argonne National Laboratory, IL 60439, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Nanophase Materials Sciences, Oak Ridge National Laboratory, Oak Ridge, TN, 37831, USA
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center for Nanophase Materials Sciences, Oak Ridge National Laboratory, Oak Ridge, Tennessee, 37831, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To unravel the relationship between trace Cu on the metal-free catalysts toward CO/CO2 reduction reaction (CO/CO2RR), we investigated the effect of trace Cu loading in N-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) on CO/CO2RR. A general trend is that increasing the Cu loading in NGQDs switches the selectivity from C1 (CH4) to C2 products in CORR. When 2.5 μg/cm2 Cu with the atomic size is loaded on NGQDs, the selectivity shifts from 62% Faradaic efficiency (FE) of CH4 to 52% FE of C2 products in CORR. Further increasing the atomic Cu loading to 3.8 μg/cm2 promotes the FE of C2 products to 78%. CO2RR requires one order of magnitude higher Cu loading than CORR to switch the selectivity from C1 to C2 products due to the low partial pressure of CO. This study clarifies the distinct impact of trace (ppm level) Cu on the activity/selectivity between CORR and CO2RR.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dioxide (CO2) removal and utilization have drawn extensive attention to reverse the negative environmental impact of CO2 emissions [1,2]. In this context, the electrochemical CO2 reduction reaction (CO2RR) utilizing renewable energy is a promising approach to generating value-added chemicals and fuels under mild operating conditions while simultaneously mitigating CO2 emissions [3–7]. However, the conversion cost is still high for practical applications due to the lack of efficient, selective, and durable catalysts [8–11]. The state-of-the-art catalysts for CO2RR are still metallic materials [12–15]. Cu stands out as the only known metal catalyst that can convert CO2 to C1–C2 hydrocarbons (such as CH4 and C2H4) and C2+ oxygenates (such as CH3COOH and C2H5OH) beyond the 2e− reduction process [16–20]. On the other hand, heteroatoms (e.g., sulfur, nitrogen, and oxygen) doped nanostructured carbon materials have been under development as a category of metal-free electrocatalysts for CO/CO2 reduction due to their large specific surface area and tunable electronic structure [21–26]. Although most heteroatoms doped carbon materials can directly convert CO2 via a 2e− reaction pathway to generate products such as CO on N-doped carbon nanotube [27] and graphene foam [28], and HCOO− on boron-doped graphene catalysts [29], a few N-doped carbon nanostructures were reported to produce high-order products such as acetate (CH3COO−) on N-doped nanodiamond [30], and C2H5OH on boron and nitrogen co-doped nanodiamond [31]. Our previous work found that N-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) with functionalized electron-donating groups (e.g., –OH and –NH2) could convert CO2 to CH4 with simultaneously high selectivity and production rate (70.0% Faradaic efficiency (FE) of CH4 and partial current density of 200 mA/cm2 at around −0.85 V vs. reversible hydrogen electrode (vs. RHE throughout the text)) [32]. Meanwhile, the functionalized NGQDs could convert CO to CH4 with a FE of around 62% and a partial current density of 120 mA/cm2 at around −0.85 V.

A trace (ppm level) of Cu has a significant effect on the electrochemical activity and selectivity of carbon catalysts for CO2RR. An early study shows that graphene oxide (GO) containing 0.002 μg/cm2 generated 10% FE of CO in CO2RR [33]. However, electrodeposited Cu nanoparticles at a trace level of 0.0423 μg/cm2 onto GO could trigger CH4 formation with about 10% FE and increasing Cu nanoparticles (average size of 8.6 nm) to the level of 0.651 μg/cm2 raised CH4 FE to 40%. No C2+ products were observed from CO2RR on Cu nanoparticles electrodeposited GO. Graphdiyne (GDY) supported Cu catalysts with loadings at the microgram per cm2 level show loading-dependent selectivity for CORR [34]. The major product was CH4 with a FE of 57.3% at −1.0 V and the minor C2 products had a FE of only 20% over the single-atom Cu on GDY with a loading of 7.5 μg/cm2. In contrast, the FE of C2 products surged to 80% at −1.0 V, and the CH4 almost disappeared when the subnanometric Cu clusters emerged as the Cu loading increased to 226 μg/cm2. Consequently, there exists a concern about the reactivity contribution of trace Cu to the CO/CO2RR when evaluating the performance of metal-free electrocatalysts.

Herein, we systemically investigated the effect of trace Cu on the activity and selectivity of functionalized NGQDs toward CO/CO2RR. We found that adding trace Cu in NGQDs could shift the selectivity from C1 to C2 products in CO/CO2RR, particularly in CORR. The FE of CH4 decreases from 62% to 10% in CORR at −0.85 V after 2.5 μg/cm2 Cu is intentionally added to the NGQDs-based gas diffusion electrodes (GDEs). In contrast, the FE of C2 products (C2H4, CH3COO−, and CH3CH2OH) increases from 7% to 52% in CORR, accompanied by a partial current density increase from 13 to 250 mA/cm2 at −0.85 V. Further increasing the atomic Cu loading to 3.8 μg/cm2 promotes the FE of C2 products to 78%, of which the major product acetate accounts for 44.4%. The partial current density of C2 products rises with the increase of Cu content at the same potential, and the highest mass activity of C2 products generation reaches 124 A/mg1 Cu at −0.90 V in CORR. This work clarifies the role of trace Cu in NGQDs-driven CO/CO2RR and proposes a competition between NGQDs and atomically dispersed Cu for electrocatalysis.

The pristine NGQDs (p-NGQDs) were synthesized following a typical route in this work [32,35]. In brief, 1,3,6-trinitropyrene was obtained from pyrene and HNO3. Then, 0.10 g of the as-prepared1,3,6-trinitropyrene was dispersed in a mixture of NaOH and deionization (DI) water under stirring. The suspension solution was then transferred to an autoclave, heated to 180 °C, and maintained for 12 h. Afterward, the autoclave was naturally cooled down to room temperature. The mixture solution after the reaction was filtered to remove byproduct solids to obtain the p-NGQDs solution. The p-NGQDs suspension was dried and obtained after dialysis for three days. The reduced NGQDs (r-NGQDs) were prepared via a typical hydrothermal method. Briefly, 20.0 mg of the p-NGQDs powder was dispersed in a mixed solution of 5.0 mL DI water and 25.0 mL dimethylformamide (DMF). The suspension solution was mixed for 20 min in an ultrasonication bath. Then, the suspension was transferred to a 50 mL autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 10 h. The as-prepared r-NGQDs suspension was then filtered in an identical process to p-NGQDs. Then, the obtained solid was dried at 80 °C in a vacuum to get the r-NGQDs powder. r-NGQDs possess the main –OH functional group and were used throughout this project. For simplicity, NGQDs are referred to r-NGQDs in the following text unless otherwise stated.

The catalyst ink was made of 20.0 mg of the NGQDs powder, 56.0 μL of the Nafion solution (Sigma, Nafion 117, 5 wt%), and solvent of isopropanol (5.0 mL) and water (5.0 mL). The as-prepared catalyst ink was sonicated for 30 min before spray-coating onto a gas diffusion electrode (GDE, Sigracet 39BB, 40.0 cm2), and the NGQDs loading on the GDE is around 0.5 mg/cm2. Subsequently, the NGQDs loaded GDE was cut into 10 pieces (4 cm2 each with 1 cm2 active area in CO/CO2RR), and the obtained small NGQDs loaded GDEs were impregnated into 10 mM CuCl2 solution with different times to add trace Cu onto the electrodes. The Cu content on each electrode was determined by the inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-MS, PerkinElmer-Optima 2000DV), which are summarized in Table S1. The p-NGQDs loaded on GDEs were prepared in an identical way as a control experiment. In this work, the NGQD-Cu2.5 electrode refers to the Cu content in the electrode as 2.5 μg/cm2, and all other electrodes were named based on the Cu content.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was used to determine the microstructure of the NGQD-Cu2.5 electrode after the experiment in CORR, and the images were obtained in a Carl Zeiss Merlin instrument operating at 1 kV. Energy Dispersive X-Ray Spectroscopy (EDS) elemental mapping images have been acquired for the same sample with a system from Bruker Nano Gmbh using an XFlash detector 5030 at 10 kV.

Aberration-corrected scanning transmission electron microscope (AC-STEM) were applied to determine the Cu structure on NGQD-Cu2.5 electrode after CORR. The catalyst layer from the electrode was scraped carefully for the characterization. High-angle annular dark field (HAADF) and secondary electron (SE) images were recorded simultaneously using a Cs-probe corrected JEOL NEOARM electron microscope (JEOL Ltd.) operated at 80 kV with semi-convergence angle of 6.5 mrad and probe current of ∼40 pA.

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were obtained using an ESCALAB Xi+ (Thermo Fisher Scientiﬁc) spectrometer utilizing a monochromatic Al Kα source operating at a power of 200 W [36,37]. The electrodes with different Cu loadings after experiments in CORR were placed on double-sided conductive carbon tape before being introduced into the ultra-high vacuum chamber of the instrument. The survey and high-resolution spectra were acquired at pass energies of 150 eV and 20 eV, respectively. For the high-resolution spectra of Cu, the scan rate is 0.1 eV with a scan number of 20. No charge neutralization was used during the measurements and charge correction was done, where necessary, by shifting the peaks relative to the adventitious C 1s at the binding energy of 284.8 eV.

X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) measurements of electrodes with different Cu loadings after experiments in CORR were performed with a fluorescence mode using a Si(111) double crystal monochromator at the 20-ID-B beamline of the Advanced Photon Sources (APS). The monochromatic X-ray beam was calibrated to the peak of the first derivative of X-ray absorption near-edge structure (XANES) at Cu K-edge from Cu foil. The XANES was extracted using Demeter, a software package for analyzing X-ray absorption spectroscopy, according to the standard analysis procedure [38].

ICP-MS was employed to determine the Cu content on each electrode. First, more than 20 mg samples were submerged in the 500 μL concentrated nitric acid in a digestion vial. Subsequently, the digestion vial was before digested at 65 °C for 30 min, followed by digestion at 130 °C for 1 h. After cooling to room temperature, 200 μL of the H2O2 (30% v/v) was added to the vial. Then, the sample was further digested at 130 °C for 1 more h, followed at 70 °C overnight, and then at 150 °C for 1 h. Next, added 200 μL of H2O2 and heated at 130 °C for 10 min. Diluted the sample volume to 10 mL and added internal standards (Yttrium, High Purity Standards Inc.) before the quantification. The ICP-MS results were normalized by the sample weight to get the final Cu content in the unit of ng/g1.

A homemade flow cell with 1 M KOH electrolyte was employed for CO/CO2 electrochemical reduction at ambient conditions. FAA-3-PK-75 anion exchange membrane (Fuel Cell Store) was used to separate anodic and cathodic compartments. Ni foam was applied as the anode, and the prepared GDE has a 4 cm2 (2 cm × 2 cm) geometric area with an active reaction area of 1 cm2 (1 cm × 1 cm). The Hg/HgCl (saturated KCl) was used as the reference electrode. The catholyte (1 M KOH) and anolyte (1 M KOH) with a flow rate of 0.5 mL/min1 were both controlled by a peristaltic pump (Harvard apparatus, P70-7000). The cell current was controlled in a galvanostatic mode, while the cathode potential was simultaneously measured by the multichannel function of EnergyLab XM (Solartron Analytical). The reported current density is the geometric current density unless otherwise stated. The cathode potential was measured online relative to the reference electrode. All potentials were converted to the RHE scale using: E RHE = E Hg/HgCl + 0.244 V + 0.0591 × pH. An iR compensation was determined by potentiostatic electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). In CORR, the CO feedstock with a flow rate of 20.00 sccm was controlled by a mass flow controller (MFC, Alicat Scientific MC). The gas product stream was injected into GC (Agilent 7890B) for quantitative analysis. In CO2RR, The CO2 feedstock with a flow rate of 20.00 sccm was controlled by a MFC (Alicat Scientific MC). The gas product stream was mixed with a constant stream of Ar (10.00 sccm) at the electrolyzer outlet and then injected into the GC for quantitative analysis. Since the CO2 flow rate in the outlet is not the same as in the inlet, the CO2 outlet flow rate was calibrated by the Ar stream according to our previous work [39]. The FE of each gas product was calculated based on the CO and CO2 outlet flow rate. Meanwhile, the catholyte was collected after electrolysis in CO2RR followed by quantifying the liquid products via 1H NMR (Bruker AV 400 MHz spectrometer). 500 μL of the catholyte was mixed internal standard of 3-(Trimethylsilyl) propionic-2,2,3,3-d4 acid sodium salt in D2O. The standard deviations were calculated based on the measurement of three independent experiments. Additionally, the anolyte and catholyte were collected and mixed after electrolysis in CORR followed by the same measurement as CO2RR, as acetate (CH3COO−) was found to migrate from the cathode to anode side.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The performance of CORR over a trace Cu loaded NGQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of NGQDs with intentionally added trace Cu in a loading of 2.5 μg/cm2 (denoted as NGQD-Cu2.5) for CORR was first evaluated in a flow cell. NGQD-Cu2.5 based GDEs exhibit prosperous activity toward the formation of C2 products, including CH3COO−, C2H4, and CH3CH2OH, with FE of >40% in the potential window of −0.70 to −0.90 V. The predominant C2 product is CH3COO− across the studied potential range. The highest FE of C2 products is 58% at −0.76 V where the FE of CH3COO− also achieves the maximum of 33.8%. CH4 is the minor product from CORR over NGQD-Cu2.5. The maximum FE of CH4 only reaches 11.3% as the potential negatively sweeps to −0.87 V. The partial current density for both C2 products and CH4 increases as the potential is swept cathodically. The jC2 climbs to 310 mA/cm2 at −0.90 V (Fig. 1 c), corresponding to a mass specific current density of 124 A/mg1 Cu, which is the highest reported value in the literature [40–42]. It should be noted that the total FE of products is less than 100% (60–95%), and the similar total FE in the range of 60–90% was also reported by other researchers [43–45], as the error is large in the flow cell for CORR, especially at low current density.

To verify that carbon-based products originate from CORR catalyzed by the NGQD-Cu2.5 electrodes, a control experiment was carried out by alternately supplying CO and Ar feedstocks to the cathode chamber of the flow cell. During the Ar supply, HER exclusively occurs at around −0.76 V, with a total current density of 280 mA/cm2, and no carbon-based products are observed (Fig. S3). Upon switching to the CO feedstock, HER is dramatically suppressed, whereas the CO reduction products, particularly the predominant C2 products, are detected (Fig. S3). The total highest FE in Fig. 1 is close to 90%, as the error is large in flow specifically at low current density.

In contrast, the blank NGQDs loaded GDEs exhibit prevalent activity to C1 hydrocarbon product (CH4) with a peak FE of 62% at −0.85 V as shown in Fig. S1a [32]. It's noteworthy that the blank NGQDs loaded GDE (NGQD-Cu0.056 electrode) contains contamination Cu with a content of 6.5 ppm, corresponding to a loading of 0.056 μg/cm2. A portion of this contamination Cu comes from the GDL substrate, which has a trace Cu content of 1.9 ppm, corresponding to a loading of 0.016 μg/cm2 (Table S1). HER prevails over the original GDL and only trace CH4 with FE < 2% is detected (Fig. S4). The activity and selectivity of NGQDs is very sensitive to the electronic structure of the functional group. The electron-donating functional group is essential to drive CORR to CH4, while the electron-withdrawing functional group prefers HER [32]. We assessed the activity of the p-NGQDs (the p-NGQDs refers to pristine NGQDs containing major electron-withdrawing –COOH functional group, see detail in the experimental section), the counterpart of NGQDs containing main –OH functional group. p-NGQDs GDEs contain a similar Cu content (6 ppm, equivalent to a loading of 0.051 μg/cm2, Table S1) as NGQD-Cu0.056 electrodes because the identical procedure was used in the preparation of those electrodes. The p-NGQDs GDEs show predominant selectivity toward HER and only yield FE < 2% toward CH4 (Fig. S5) in CORR, similar to the blank GDLs. The characterization (XPS, Raman, FTIR, and HRTEM) of p-NGQDs and NGQDs were shown in our previous work [32], and p-NGQDs stand for the p-GQD and NGQDs refer to the r-GQD in that work. The XPS of N, Raman, and FTIR characterization of p-NGQDs and NGQDs are regenerated and shown in Figs. S6 and S7. Thus, the activity of NGQDs loaded GDE comes from NGQDs rather than the trace Cu with a loading of 0.056 μg/cm2. However, the Cu competes with NGQDs and shifts the selectivity of CORR as the Cu loading increases to 2.5 μg/cm2 although it is still at a trace level.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of Cu loadings on the selectivity in CORR
ce:section.ce:para: To systematically investigate the effect of Cu loadings on the selectivity in CORR, we prepared NGQDs-based GDEs including a wide range of Cu loadings, as shown in Table S1. The performance of NGQDs GDEs with different Cu loadings for CORR is shown in Fig. 2 . Both FE and partial current density of C2 products increase with the increase of Cu loading (Fig. 2a and b), while the activity and selectivity toward CH4 exhibit a reverse trend. Specifically, the maximum FEs of C2 products are all close to 80% in the potential range of −0.65 to −0.75 V for electrodes with a Cu loading of 3.5 μg/cm2 and beyond. In comparison, the highest FEs of C2 products are only around 15% for both NGQD-Cu0.056 and NGQD-Cu0.36 electrodes at the same potential range (Fig. 2a). The jC2 is only around 4 mA/cm2 at −0.66 V for the NGQD-Cu0.056 electrode, while the jC2 rises to 204 mA/cm2 at the same potential for the NGQD-Cu3.8 electrode (Fig. 2b), suggesting that jC2 increases by 50 times after adding 3.8 μg/cm2 Cu into the electrode.

On the contrary, the highest FE of CH4 reaches 62% for the NGQD-Cu0.056 electrode at −0.81 ∼ −0.85 V. As the Cu loading increases to 2.5 μg/cm2, the highest FE of CH4 declines to less than 10% for the NGQD-Cu2.5 electrode (Fig. 2c). NGQD-Cu0.056 electrode possesses the highest partial current density of CH4 among all studied electrodes (Fig. 2d). More details about FEs of all CORR products on different electrodes can be found in Fig. S1. Since the same NGQDs catalyst was used in all electrodes, the switching of selectivity and activity from C1 to C2 products in CORR is attributed to added trace Cu. This finding indicates that the trace Cu (ppm level) added to the NGQDs GDEs switches the reaction pathway of CORR.

The product distribution of CORR as a function of the trace Cu loading is further plotted in Fig. 3 a. The FEs of CORR products for different Cu loading are compared at the same current density of around 150 mA/cm2. The FE of CH4 drops to less than 10% when the Cu content is higher than 2.5 μg/cm2. The FE of CH3CH2OH is relatively stable (around 5%) and is independent of Cu loading. FEs of both CH3COO− and C2H4 increase until the Cu loading rises to 3.5 μg/cm2 and then keep relatively stable afterward. CH3COO− is the predominant product of C2 products. The NGQD-Cu3.8 exhibits CH3COO− FE of 44.4% and a partial current density of 117 mA/cm2 at −0.67 V. The CH3COO− as the predominant product in CORR was reported on tailored Cu catalysts with loadings of several hundred μg/cm2 [45–47]. Cu nanocube catalyst with a loading of 500 μg/cm2 delivered a maximal CH3COO− FE of 43% and partial current density of 200 mA/cm2 at around −2.0 V without iR correction [46]. Cu nanosheets with (111) facet at the loading of 500 μg/cm2 exhibited a CH3COO− FE of 48% with a partial current density up to 131 mA/cm2 at −0.75 V [45]. Compared to reported results, the NGQDs catalyst with trace Cu is promising for CH3COO− production. Fig. 3b presents a noticeable trend that the FE of CH4 decreases while the FE of C2 products increases with the increase of Cu loading. The FE ratio of CH4/C2 is 9.5 for NGQD-Cu0.056 electrode, and it drops to 0.02 for NGQD-Cu3.8 electrode, indicating the trace Cu loading significantly shifts the product selectivity and activity of CORR.

As a control experiment, the CORR on commercial 25 nm Cu nanoparticles (NPs) with a loading of 3.1 μg/cm2 was evaluated under identical conditions (Fig. S8). Overall, the commercial Cu NPs exhibit poor performance for CORR. The total FE of carbon products is less than 40% at the operating potentials from −0.67 to −0.81 V. The predominant product shifts from C2 to CH4 as the potential increases to −0.73 V. The NGQD-Cu3.5 GDE with a similar Cu loading (3.5 μg/cm2) shows a much higher total FE of carbon products (78%) than commercial Cu NPs GDE (20%) under the same current density of 150 mA/cm2. In contrast to the predominant product of CH3COO− (FE of 38%) on NGQD-Cu3.5 GDEs at −0.77 V, the main product is CH4 with a FE of 12% on commercial Cu NPs GDE with the similar Cu loading and at the same potential. The improvement of activity and selectivity toward C2 products on NGQD-Cu GDEs compared with the commercial Cu is attributed to the enhanced dispersion of trace Cu on NGQDs and the potential synergy between NGQD and dispersed Cu.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The performance of CO2RR over trace Cu loaded NGQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of CO2RR over trace Cu loaded NGQDs was investigated in a similar way as CORR. CO2RR shows a general performance trend with the Cu loading similar to CORR (Fig. S2). The partial current density and FE of C2 products increase with the Cu loading, and the potential shifts positively with the Cu loading. The CH4 exhibits a reverse change of activity and selectivity with the increase of Cu loading. However, the switching from C1 to C2 in CO2RR is not as sensitive to Cu loading as in CORR. The FE of C2 exceeds that of CH4 in CORR at a Cu loading of 1.9 μg/cm2 (Fig. S1c). However, the CH4 predominates in CO2RR until the Cu loading increases to 14.1 μg/cm2 (Fig. S2b and c), indicating one order of magnitude higher Cu loading than CORR is required to switch the selectivity from C1 to C2 products in CO2RR. These results indicate that switching from C1 to C2 in CO2RR is much more difficult than in CORR, and the possible reason is that high partial pressure of CO is required for the C–C coupling on the trace Cu. Additionally, the C2H4 is the predominant C2 product instead of CH3COO− on the NGQD-Cu14.1 electrode in CO2RR, indicating the difference in reaction pathways to C2 products between CO2RR and CORR.

In order to correlate the structure and oxidation status with the performance of CORR/CO2RR, the electrodes after CORR were characterized via different instruments. No Cu particles are found based on SEM images as shown in Fig. S10 on the after NGQD-Cu2.5 electrode, as the Cu content is too low, or the Cu particle size is too small. Fig. S11 and Table S2 display the EDX mapping and analysis of NGQD-Cu2.5 electrode. Since the characterization of ppm level Cu in carbon material is a huge challenge, the Cu structure on the after NGQD-Cu2.5 electrode was determined further with AC-STEM as shown in Fig. 4 . No metal nanoparticles or clusters are observed and only Cu single atoms are found as highlighted by red circles in Fig. 4(b), indicating the Cu atoms are atomically dispersed in NGQDs electrodes.

XPS in Fig. 5 a exhibits identical peaks at ca. 932.6 eV for Cu 2p3/2 for NGQD-Cu2.5, NGQD-Cu3.5, and NGQD-Cu3.8 electrodes, which is situated between Cu0 (932.4 eV) and Cu2+ (934.6 eV) [48–50], indicating the ionic Cuδ+ (0 < δ < 2) nature of copper. For the NGQD-Cu14.1 sample, the Cu 2p3/2 peak shifts to higher energy at ca. 932.9 eV and a strong peak at ca. 941.0 eV associated with the Cu2+ satellite is observed (Fig. S9). No pronounced Cu peaks are observed for NGQD-Cu0.056, NGQD-Cu0.36, and NGQD-Cu1.9 electrodes due to the shallow concentration of Cu [33]. XAS was performed to investigate the chemical structure of Cu species further. NGQD-Cu2.5 and NGQD-Cu3.8 electrodes show higher Cu K-edge energy than the NGQD-Cu0.056 electrode (Fig. 5b). That is because the Cu species in NGQD-Cu2.5 and NGQD-Cu3.8 electrodes are mainly exposed on the surface of NGQDs layer due to the impregnation method, and they are more susceptible to be oxidized by oxygen in the air. By contrast, the Cu species in the NGQD-Cu0.056 is mainly under the NGQDs layer, as the Cu in the NGQD-Cu0.056 is mostly introduced from the blank GDL and environment during the electrode preparation. According to the Cu K-edge normalized XANES spectra, the energy absorption from all three electrodes is in the range between Cu2O and CuO, suggesting Cu in electrodes with chemical state of Cuδ+ (1 < δ < 2). Fig. 5c shows Cu Fourier-transformed K3-weighted extended X-ray absorption fine structure (EXAFS) in R space for NGQD-Cu electrodes. Only one notable peak at ca. 1.5 Å is seen for NGQD-Cu2.5 and NGQD-Cu3.8, and no obvious peak is observed for NGQD-Cu0.056 electrode. The peak at 1.5 Å is contributed to Cu–C/O scattering [34,49,51], which confirms that the Cu atoms are atomically dispersed in NGQDs electrodes. According to the characterization above, we can conclude that atomically dispersed Cu exists with an oxidation state of Cuδ+ (1 < δ < 2) in the trace Cu-loaded NGQDs, leading to the CORR selectivity shift.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed mechanism for the selectivity shift in CORR
ce:section.ce:para: We envision that atomically dispersed Cu with a trace loading shifts the selectivity from C1 to C2 products in CORR. The activity and selectivity of CORR were reported to be dependent on the atomic level Cu size in GDY-supported Cu catalysts [34]. The major product is CH4 with a FE of 57.3%, while the FE of C2 products is only 20% with single-atom Cu (loading of 7.5 μg/cm2) on GDY at −1.0 V. The FE of C2 products climbs to 80% at −1.0 V, while the FE of C1 is almost eliminated when the subnanometric clusters Cu emerge at a loading of 226 μg/cm2 even though the atomically dispersed Cu is still predominant based on XAS analysis. However, GDY-supported Cu catalysts exhibit poor performance for CO2RR. The predominant product is H2 (FE of 90%) with the single-atom Cu at −1.0 V. The poor performance of CO2RR was attributed to the great population of low coordinated single-site Cu resulting in stronger chemisorption of H∗ vs. CO2 as compared to bulk Cu. In contrast, in CORR, the single-site Cu or subnanometric cluster sites facilitate protonation of CO∗ to CHO∗ or OCCO∗ due to strong chemisorption of H∗ and high partial pressure of CO. Another study reported around 60% FE of C2+ products and negligible CH4 in CORR on 6.9 wt% atomically Cu atoms (69 μg/cm2) dispersing on nitrogen-rich porous carbon (Cu–N–C) catalyst [52]. CH3COO− as the main C2+ product achieved a maximum 30% FE at a partial current density of 48 mA/cm2. CH3COO− was proposed to form via Eley-Rideal mechanism (C–C coupling between one free CO molecule and one adsorbed ∗CO on the Cu surface) instead of the Langmuir Hinshelwood mechanism (C–C coupling between two adsorbed ∗CO). Recently, CH3COO− was reported to form via the hydrogenation of CHCO∗ to CH2CO∗ and then the detach of CH2CO∗ by the following nucleophilic attack from water via both the Eley-Rideal and Langmuir Hinshelwood mechanisms during CORR [53].

We have attempted to propose the effect of trace Cu in NGQD on CORR based on our experimental observations and documented theoretical investigations. The predominant product is CH4 with blank NGQD (NGQD-Cu0.056) in both CORR and CO2RR. That's because electron-donating functional groups on NGQDs facilitate the adsorption of CO2 by high charge density and stabilize the intermediate of CO∗ for hydrogenation, while a limited number of neighboring active site (e.g., N dopant) pairs restrains C–C coupling. After atomic Cu atoms are dispersed on the NGQD surface, the selectivity of products is switched from C1 to C2 product due to the change in reaction pathway. The C–C coupling starts to occur on the Cu atoms, and C2 product is easier to form in CORR than CO2RR, as C–C coupling is more feasible to occur by the Eley-Rideal mechanism due to the high partial pressure of CO. In another scenario, Cu atomic catalyst can incorporate with a neighboring active site (most probably a N dopant) in NGQDs to drive C–C coupling through the Langmuir Hinshelwood mechanism, leading to C2 product generation.

In conclusion, we demonstrated that adding trace Cu loading into NGQDs shifts the selectivity from C1 hydrocarbon to C2 products in CORR. Blank NGQDs mainly generate CH4 with a maximum FE of 62%. Adding 3.8 μg/cm2 atomic Cu into NGQDs strongly suppresses CH4 formation while promoting the formation of C2 products to reach a FE as high as 78%, of which the major product acetate accounts for 44.4%. The highest mass activity of C2 products reaches 124 A mg−1 Cu at −0.90 V in CORR. The selectivity switching in CO2RR is not as sensitive to Cu loading as in CORR. The Cu loading required for CO2RR is almost ten times of that for CORR in order to switch the selectivity from C1 to C2 products. The difference in Cu loading requirement originates from the difference in CO partial pressure between CORR and CO2RR, resulting in divergent reaction mechanism of C–C coupling.

Xiang Lyu: data curation, formal analysis, investigation, methodology, writing-original draft; Tianyu Zhang and Zhengyuan Li: data curation and formal analysis, Writing-review & editing; Charl J. Jafta and Alexey Serov: resources and data curation, Writing-review & editing; In-hui Hwang and Chengjun Sun: resources and data curation; David A. Cullen: resources and data curation; Jianlin Li: funding acquisition supervision, writing-review & editing; Jingjie Wu: conceptualization, supervision, funding acquisition, and Writing-review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is partially supported by the Office of Fossil Energy and Carbon Management of the U.S. Department of Energy under Award Number DE-FE0031919. This research used resources of the Advanced Photon Source, an Office of Science User Facility operated for the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) Office of Science by Argonne National Laboratory and was supported by the U.S. DOE under Contract No. DE-AC02-06CH11357, and the Canadian Light Source and its funding partners.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Xu, D. Rebollar, H. He, L. Chong, Y. Liu, C. Liu, C.-J. Sun, T. Li, J.V. Muntean, R.E. Winans, Highly selective electrocatalytic CO2 reduction to ethanol by metallic clusters dynamically formed from atomically dispersed copper, Nature Energy, 5 (2020) 623-632.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chemical upgrade of carbon monoxide to acetate on an atomically dispersed copper catalyst via CO-insertion

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Fu, Y. Wang, H. Shen, Y. Yu, F. Xu, G. Zhou, W. Xie, R. Qin, C. Dun, C.-W. Pao, Chemical upgrade of carbon monoxide to acetate on an atomically dispersed copper catalyst via CO-insertion, Materials Today Physics, 19 (2021) 100418.
